segments.s290-g129: 'از' here functions partitive ('among/of'), introducing a subset: 'among those...'. 'آنها' = 'those' (not 'they') and is the noun being picked out. 'که' introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). The verb phrase shows a poetic inversion: instead of the more colloquial مانده بودند ('had remained'), Ferdowsi places the copula/auxiliary بود(ند) before the participle مانده; this yields 'who were remaining/who had remained'. Finally, 'به جای' is a locative phrase 'in their place(s)', indicating where they remained.
segments.s290-g130: The relative 'که' continues ('whose...'). 'پیرانشان' is 'their elders' (پیران + possessive suffix -شان). 'بد سر' is a terse, poetic description meaning 'bad‑headed' → 'ill‑tempered' or 'of poor disposition'; the adjective بد qualifies سر to describe temperament (the copula 'بود(ند)' is omitted here by ellipsis, as is common in poetry). The conjunction 'و' links the phrase to 'کدخدای' ('the village headman'), implying the same negative assessment applies (i.e. the headman was also bad).
segments.s290-g105: همی گفت is a habitual/progressive phrase: همی gives a continuous or iterative sense ('kept/was saying') and گفت is the past verb 'said' introducing direct speech. کای is the vocative particle 'O', and کردگار جهان is a compound noun phrase 'Maker/Creator of the world' (کردگار = Creator/God, جهان = world). The order places the vocative after the reporting phrase as the addressee of the quoted words.
segments.s290-g106: تو دانی is a present (or historical present) 2nd-person statement 'you know' (تو = you, دانی = you know). همی again adds an ongoing/emphatic sense. آشکار و نهان are two nouns/adjectives used as nouns meaning 'the manifest/clear and the hidden/secret', joined by و 'and'. The phrase attributes knowledge of both visible and hidden things to the addressee.
segments.s290-g107: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/truly'. که introduces the subordinate clause/reason 'that/since'. کاووس is the proper name (Kavus). بد کرده بود is a compound past ('had done ill'): بد is 'bad/evil', کرده is the perfect participle 'done', and بود is the past auxiliary 'was/had' giving the pluperfect sense.
segments.s290-g108: به پاداش is a prepositional phrase 'as/to/for a recompense' (به here indicating 'in return'). از او 'from him' marks the source. زهر و کین are the coordinated objects 'poison and vengeance/hatred' (زهر can be literal or figurative 'poison/venom'; کین = enmity). آزمود is the simple past 'he experienced/tasted/tried' (subject is implied from context).
segments.s290-g109: که introduces the clause 'that'. دیو (دیو + the enclitic-like element ی) denotes 'a demon' (دیو) with the following چنین 'thus/so' qualifying its action. بر سیاوش indicates direction/target 'against/upon Siavash'. گماشت is the past of گماشتن 'placed/caused/put', here 'set upon' or 'devised against'.
segments.s290-g110: ندانم is the first-person negative present/past 'I do not know' (negative prefix ن- + دانم). جز این 'except/other than this' narrows the speaker's knowledge. کینه بر دل چه داشت is an idiomatic clause 'what grudge he had in his heart' (کینه = hatred/grudge, بر دل = in/on the heart, چه داشت = what he had/what was in it).
segments.s290-g111: ولیکن is a conjunction 'but/yet'. به پیروزی uses به with an instrumental/causal sense 'by/through the victory' (به + noun). یک خدای 'one God' qualifies the agent of that victory; یک is the numeral 'one/a single' and خدای is 'God'. The whole prepositional phrase sets the basis or appeal ('by the victory of the one God').
segments.s290-g112: جهاندار is an epithet 'ruler/holder of the world' (here in apposition to God). نیکی ده is an imperative construction: نیکی (goodness) as object and ده, the archaic imperative of دادن, 'give (goodness)'. و links to رهنمای, which here functions as a petitionary verb-like imperative 'and guide (me)' or as a demand 'and be a guide' (رهنمای is literally 'guide/adviser' but in context works as imperative/request).
segments.s290-g73: کنون is an adverb meaning “now” placed at the front for temporal emphasis. The phrase پند گودرز و فرمان من is a coordinated nominal phrase: پند گودرز = “Goudarz’s advice” (possessor follows the noun in this older/poetic word order, no ezafe), و links the two nouns, and فرمان من = “my command” (من functioning as the possessor, placed after فرمان). Together they form the objects/theme that the verb in the next segment will act on.
segments.s290-g74: بیفگند is an archaic/past verbal form meaning “he cast off/put away” (third person, past); the subject is omitted and understood from context. گفتار و پیمان من are the direct objects: گفتار “words/speech” and پیمان “pledge/covenant,” joined by و; من again marks possession. Word order here is somewhat inverted (verb before its objects), typical of epic diction.
segments.s290-g75: تبه کرد is a two-word verbal sequence: تبه (here meaning ruin/corrupt) plus کرد (to do/make), yielding “made ruinous” or “spoiled.” مهر دل‌پاک را is the definite object marked with را: مهر “affection/love” modified by دل‌پاک “pure‑hearted,” i.e. “the love/affection of the pure‑hearted.” The را signals this noun phrase as the direct object of تبه کرد.
segments.s290-g76: به زهر اندر آمیخت is a prepositional/verb cluster meaning “mixed into (the) poison”: به زهر (به + noun) = “into/to poison,” اندر is a literary preposition “in/within,” and آمیخت is the past of آمیختن “to mix.” تریاک را (marked by را) is the thing mixed in: “opium.” The word order places the directional phrase before the verb and the definite object at the end, typical of the poem’s style.
segments.s290-g77: که آمد is a relative/connecting clause introduced by که = “who/that/because”; آمد is simple past “came.” به جنگ شما = “to war against you” (به with جنگ marks direction/target), and با سپاه “with an army” indicates accompaniment/instrument. The clause describes someone’s arrival for battle against “you.”
segments.s290-g78: که چندان شد is a purposive/resultive continuation: که = “so/that,” چندان شد = “became so great” or “was so much.” از شهر ایران تباه: از = “from/of,” شهر ایران = “the cities/land of Iran,” and تباه = “ruined/destroyed.” Together the clause reports the consequence: that the towns of Iran were ruined to that extent.
segments.s290-g79: ز is the poetic form of از meaning “from,” so ز توران بسیچید = “they mustered/gathered from Turan” (بسیچید is past “mustered/gathered”). و آمد دمان = “and came to the plain/edge” (آمد past “came,” دمان a topographical noun “skirt/edge/foothill/plain”). The sequence narrates movement and mustering from Turan.
segments.s290-g80: که introduces a subordinate/temporal clause (“when/that”), ژوپین گودرز = “Goudarz’s ژوپین” (ژوپین = gambeson/armour; possessor again after the noun). بودش is بود + enclitic -ش meaning “it was” or “he was (it),” here indicating existence/state in the past, and زمان “time/then” gives the temporal frame. Idiomatically this yields “when Goudarz was in his gambeson” or “at that time Goudarz wore his gambeson.”
segments.s290-g9: چنان 'thus/so' sets the manner; هم + همی together produce a classical emphatic/continuous aspect ('also/indeed' + continuous marker) indicating a continuing state; بود is the past copula 'was/ remained'; بر اسب شاه is a locative phrase 'upon the king's horse' (preposition + noun + possessor). Word order is somewhat marked/poetic: manner and aspect precede the copula, and the locative follows it.
segments.s290-g10: بدان تا functions as a purpose marker sequence meaning 'so/thereby' or 'so that'; تا introduces a purpose/result clause and governs the subjunctive; ببینند is the present-subjunctive/optative 'may/let them see' (used after تا for purpose); روی + ش is the object 'his face' (روی = face, ش = 3sg enclitic possessor); سپاه is the logical subject 'the army' — Persian word order here places the verb and object before the subject for poetic effect.
segments.s290-g11: بر + ایشان = 'to/at them' (بر used here as 'toward/to'); همی indicates repeated/continuous action; خواند is the past tense 'he uttered/recited' (here 'he called out' or 'he recited' an interjection); شاه is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb for poetic word order; آفرین is the interjection uttered ('Bravo!' / acclamation).
segments.s290-g12: که introduces the content/wish reported by the king ('that'); آباد is the predicate 'prosperous'; بادا is an optative particle meaning 'may it be' (classical wish form); به links the following noun phrase, here indicating the sphere or instrument ('by/through' or 'for'); گردان زمین 'the troop/host of the land' (گردان = troop/host, زمین = land/realm). Overall this is a wish clause: 'that may (it) be prosperous by the host of the land.'
segments.s290-g13: به آیین = 'in/according to the custom/formation' (به + آیین, 'in the manner of'); پس پشت لشکر = 'the rear/after part of the army' (پس = after/behind, پشت = rear, لشکر = army); چو کوه = simile 'like a mountain' (چو = like/as, کوه = mountain). This is a descriptive prepositional/adverbial cluster setting manner and position.
segments.s290-g14: همی رفت is the imperfective/continuous past 'kept going / proceeded'; گودرز is the proper-name subject (placed after the verb in poetic order); با آن گروه is a prepositional phrase 'with that company' (با = with, آن = that, گروه = group/company). The clause describes movement: Goudarz proceeded with that band.
segments.s290-g15: سر کشتگان = 'the heads of the slain' (سر = head, کشتگان = the slain); را is the definite/object/topic marker used to mark the phrase as definite or topical; فگنده is an archaic/poetic past participle 'thrown' or 'cast down' (classical formation); نگون is an adjective 'downcast/overthrown/ill‑fated' describing the state. The cluster presents the condition of the slain's heads.
segments.s290-g16: A triad of nouns connected by و: سلیح و تن و جامه = 'weapons and body and garments'; هاشان is the 3pl possessive enclitic 'their' modifying those items; به خون is a prepositional phrase 'in/with blood' (به + خون) indicating that those things are soaked/covered in blood. The whole segment describes the bloody condition of their arms, bodies and clothes.
segments.s290-g17: همان is a demonstrative ('those/the same') qualifying ده مبارز ('ten fighters'). کز is the literary contraction of که + از (i.e. 'that/from'), introducing a relative clause, and آوردگاه is the place 'battlefield'. The phrase therefore reads 'those ten fighters that were from the battlefield.'
segments.s290-g18: بیاورده بودند is a compound past (past participle بیاورده + auxiliary بودند) meaning 'had brought'. In this poetic word order the agent follows the verb: گردان شاه = 'the king's troop' (noun + possessor), so the full clause means 'the king's troop had brought (them)'.
segments.s290-g19: پس is a discourse connective 'then'. لشکر is the subject 'the host/army'. اندر همی is an adverbial phrase ('in/among, together' with the archaic particle همی giving an iterative/continuous flavor) and راندند is 3rd-person plural past 'they drove/advanced'. The clause means 'then the army advanced together.'
segments.s290-g20: ابر شهریار here is an honorific compound applied to the king (an intensifying epithet + 'king'). آفرین is an exclamation 'bravo/praise' and خواندند is past plural 'they uttered/sang/called out', so the clause means 'they cried “āfarīn” to the king.'
segments.s290-g21: چو is the conjunction 'when'. گودرز is the subject name. نزدیک خسرو is an adverbial phrase 'near Khosrow', and رسید is the simple past 'arrived/reached', so together: 'when Goudarz reached near Khosrow.'
segments.s290-g22: پیاده شد is an idiomatic past ('he became on-foot' = 'he dismounted/rode down'). از دور is a prepositional phrase 'from afar'. کو را is an archaic interrogative 'who' with را as the object marker, and بدید is past 'saw' — 'from afar he saw who (it was).' Note: کو here is archaic interrogative; the object marker را attaches to it.
segments.s290-g23: ستایش‌کنان is an adverbial present participle meaning 'praising' (i.e., 'while praising' or 'praisingly'). پهلوان سپاه is a poetic noun phrase 'the champion of the army' (noun + possessor), so 'the army's champion, praising...'.
segments.s290-g24: بیامد is a past tense 'he came'. بغلتید is past 'he embraced' (the prefix ب- with the root غلت/غلّت yields 'embraced'); the two verbs are coordinated (came and embraced). در پیش شاه is a locative phrase 'in the presence of the king' (i.e. 'before the king').
segments.s290-g1: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning “when,” introducing a temporal clause. The phrase از روز نه ساعت is a prepositional quantitative phrase “nine hours of the day,” and اندر گذشت is a past action: گذشت (past) = “had passed,” with اندر adding the sense “within/into.” Word order is conjunction + prepositional phrase + past verb.
segments.s290-g2: خور is the grammatical subject “the sun.” از گنبد چرخ گردان is a poetic, slightly inverted noun phrase: گنبد چرخ گردان = “the revolving dome (vault) of the sky,” with گردان as an adjectival participle “revolving.” بگشت is a past-tense verb meaning “turned/returned” (classical form with the prefix ب- plus گشت).
segments.s290-g3: جهاندار is an honorific epithet “sovereign/world-ruler” placed before the name خسرو in apposition. به نزد سپاه is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning “to/into the presence of the army” (به + نزد = toward/in the sight of).
segments.s290-g4: بیامد is simple past “came” (3rd sing.). The b + دان tokens are split here though they form the demonstrative/ prepositional sense بدان (= “to that”); the supplied lemma senses do not include this use for دان. دشت آوردگاه is appositive: دشت “plain” and آوردگاه “battlefield/place of gathering for battle.”
segments.s290-g5: پذیره شدندش is a classical/periphrastic reception construction: پذیره (reception/received) together with the auxiliary/verb شدند (they became/did) plus the enclitic -ش (him) yields the sense “they received him” / “he was received.” سراسر modifies سران: “throughout/by all the leaders.”
segments.s290-g6: همه is a universal quantifier “all,” placed before two coordinated nouns. نامداران is used as a noun-group “the famed/renowned (men),” و links to جنگاوران “warriors/fighters”; the phrase lists those who welcomed him.
segments.s290-g7: بر او خواندند literally “they sang/recited upon him” — بر + pronoun او indicates the addressee/target of the acclamation. خواندند is past plural “they recited/sang,” and آفرین is the acclamation/praise being sung. بخردان indicates the group “by/from the wise” or addressing the wise; the construction means they chanted this praise in the circle of the wise.
segments.s290-g8: که‌ای is an archaic/exclamatory vocative-like particle (here introducing direct address). شهریار is the addressee “king,” و links to سر موبدان where سر means “chief/leader” and موبدان are the mobeds (Zoroastrian priests); together the phrase is a direct address: “O king and chief of the mobeds!”
segments.s290-g81: پسر is the main noun ('the son') and is followed by با + برادر ('with his brother'), marking accompaniment. The coordinated nouns کلاه و کمر ('helmet(s) and belt(s)') follow without a linking verb and function as attendant equipment/attire associated with the son and his brother; Persian omits a copula, so the list simply piles attributes/possessions after the participants.
segments.s290-g82: This segment continues the enumerative series: سلیح و سپاه ('weapons/armament and host/army') and then و همه بوم و بر ('and all the land and domain'). همه scopes over بوم و بر to indicate the whole territory/people. The coordination with و simply strings together possessions/forces belonging to the earlier-mentioned participants.
segments.s290-g83: بداد is a noun meaning a harmful fate/ill-treatment; از پی + noun expresses ‘on account of’ / ‘following on the heels of’ — here از marks the causal relation and پی = 'after, following (the)'. مهر افراسیاب is a genitive construction 'the love/affection of Afrasiab' that is the cause referenced by از پی.
segments.s290-g84: زمانه here personifies fortune/fate as an agent. بر او کرد is a light construction where زمانه is subject and کرد (here 'caused/put') takes the phrase که بر او بیاید; بر او = 'upon him'. چندین شتاب is the object/complement meaning 'many swift onrushes' or 'many spurts/impetuses' — چندین quantifies شتاب. The verb کرد is past tense indicating what fate brought upon him.
segments.s290-g85: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a subordinate purpose/that-clause ('so that' / 'that'). مشک و کافور ناب is a coordinated noun phrase 'musk and pure camphor' (ناب qualifies both items, 'pure/choice'). The whole phrase is the object/content of the order introduced by تا.
segments.s290-g86: به introduces the material/mixture relation ('with/into/for') and عنبر is the noun 'amber(gris)'. برآمیخته is a participle/adjective meaning 'mingled/infused' describing how the musk and camphor are to be treated. با گلاب attaches as an adjunct 'with rosewater' — the whole phrase says that the musk and camphor should be mingled into/with amber and rosewater.
segments.s290-g87: تنش را places 'his body' as a definite object (را). بیالود here functions as a stative/past notion 'made unstained/purified' (an older/poetic adjective/verb-form). زان سر به سر is an intensive adverbial phrase 'from that head right through (i.e. from head to toe / entirely)', literally 'from that head to head', emphasizing completeness.
segments.s290-g88: به introduces the instrument/material ('with' or 'by means of') and links to کافور و مشکش ('camphor and his musk' — مشکش = مشک + possessive suffix). بیاگند is an older/poetic verb form; although its basic lexical sense can be 'to putrefy'/'to corrupt', here in context it is used transitively to mean 'to impregnate or affect with a smell' (i.e. to anoint/imbue the head with the scent). سر is the affected part 'the head'.
segments.s290-g49: The clause starts with یکی (a certain/someone) as the subject, followed by داستان زد, a poetic idiom where زد (literally 'struck') is used to mean 'told/uttered' a tale. پس از (literally 'after of') marks the temporal phrase 'after his death' with از as the preposition 'from/of' and اوی as an archaic/genitive pronoun 'his'.
segments.s290-g50: This is an instrumental/ablative phrase: به خون indicates 'with/by means of blood' (به used instrumentally), دو دیده 'two eyes' is the noun phrase, and بیالود is a poetic/perfective verb meaning 'bedewed / stained' (an older poetic form); روی is the direct object 'face'. The whole phrase modifies the preceding clause, describing the action done after his death.
segments.s290-g51: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'); بخت بد است is the copular clause 'ill fortune is ...', where بخت is 'fate/fortune' and بد 'bad/ill'. اژدهای دژم is the predicate nominal 'a grim/sullen dragon' (دژم an epithet meaning 'grim/sullen').
segments.s290-g52: به دام آورد is a light-verb construction: به دام (into/into the trap) + آورد ('brought/caused' but here 'caught/ensnared'). شیر شرزه is the object 'the rebellious/insolent lion' (شرزه an epithet). The final به دم is an instrumental/locative phrase 'by the tail / at the tip' (به used for 'by' or 'at'), describing how it was caught.
segments.s290-g53: This negative/generalizing statement centers on نیابد (finds/attains) used impersonally: به مردی 'for a man' (or 'as for a man'), نیابد کسی literally 'no one finds/achieves', and ز + و + رها form the phrase 'free from it/him' (ز = 'from', و here enclitic/pronoun = 'him/it' in older orthography, followed by رها 'free'). The construction expresses that no one can free a man from that danger.
segments.s290-g54: چنین آمد is a short main clause 'thus came' (چنین = 'thus/so', آمد = past 'came'); این tiezچنگ اژدها is the subject phrase 'this sharp-clawed dragon', where تیزچنگ is an epithet meaning 'sharp-clawed' rather than a proper name in this line, and اژدها = 'dragon'.
segments.s290-g55: کشیدی is 2nd-person past perfective of کشیدن and here means 'you bore/experienced/kept' (used idiomatically as 'you bore/kept watch over'); همه‌ساله is an adverbial 'yearly / every year'; تیمار من is the object 'my care' (تیمار = 'care, solicitude'). Word order is Subject (you implied) + verb + adverbial + object.
segments.s290-g56: میان بسته بودی is a compound describing a past state: میان 'in the middle/among', بسته 'bound/tied' (past participle/adjective), بودی 'you were' (past). The phrase as a whole means 'you had been bound/closed in the midst'. به پیکار من is a prepositional/possessive phrase 'into/for my battle' (به = 'to/for', پیکار = 'battle', من = possessive 'my' in context).
segments.s290-g97: چنین است forms an emphatic copular phrase ('such is ...'); کردار is the noun being described ('conduct, behavior') and این پر فریب is a nominal phrase modifying کردار: این = 'this' and پر فریب is a compound adjective where پر = 'full (of)' + فریب = 'deception', i.e. 'this one full of deceit'. Word order places the copula and emphatic particle before the noun phrase for rhetorical effect.
segments.s290-g98: This is an exclamatory clause: چه مایه = 'what an amount/measure' (چه as exclamative + مایه = 'amount/measure'), فراز است = 'there is height/ascent' with the copula است; و links to the next noun phrase. چندی نشیب uses چندی as an interrogative/adverbial 'how much/how long' modifying نشیب ('descent, fall'). Overall: 'what heights there are and how long the falls.'
segments.s290-g99: خردمند را دل is a topicalized phrase: خردمند marked by را as topic/emphasis and دل ('the heart') is the theme; ز introduces the cause ('because of') and کردار اوی is 'his conduct' with اوی an archaic possessive 'his'. So: 'the wise man's heart, from/because of his conduct...'.
segments.s290-g100: بماند (past) = 'remained/was left'; همی is a frequentative/durative particle giving an ongoing sense ('kept/continued to'); خیره is a predicative adjective 'astonished, stupefied'; از marks the object/cause ('at/by') and کار اوی = 'his deed(s)'. The verb precedes the adjective (common in classical style): 'remained continuously astonished at his deeds.'
segments.s290-g101: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that'; گروی زره را is the definite object marked by را (here 'گروی' appears as a noun modifying زره, likely 'buckle/clasp of the armor' though that specific sense is not given in the provided list); بدید is the past tense 'saw'.
segments.s290-g102: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / a'; باد سرد = 'a cold wind' (subject); از جگر 'from the jigar/liver' uses a visceral organ idiomatically for interior feeling; برکشید is past 'rose up / was lifted' (here intransitive sense 'rose up'). The whole phrase conveys a sudden chill or dread rising in him.
segments.s290-g103: نگه کرد is the compound verb 'نگه کردن' in past tense 'looked'; here the verb precedes the subject (خسرو) for stylistic word order. بدان is the classical 'به + آن' = 'at that' (written here as ب + دان); دان in the given senses list does not match this demonstrative use (see note). زشت = 'ugly' modifies روی = 'face'. So 'Khosrow looked at that ugly face.'
segments.s290-g104: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; دیوی = 'like a demon'. The following phrase به سر بر فروهشته موی is a prepositional description: به سر = 'on (the) head', بر before فروهشته intensifies the directional/locative sense, فروهشته is a participle 'cast down/let fall' modifying موی 'hair' — 'hair fallen down upon the head'. The small particle ی after دیو can be read as an archaic copular/linking element in this poetic line.
segments.s290-g33: ز here is the poetic preposition 'by / from' marking the agent; دادار is 'the Creator' and is the agent of the praising action that comes later. بر + پهلوان formulates the target of the praise ('upon/the champion'), and آفرین is the noun/interjection 'praise, acclamation' that will be 'read/uttered' by the agent.
segments.s290-g34: همی + خواند is the classical/historic present/habitual construction: همی before a past-form verb gives the sense 'kept on / would' — here 'would utter/say'. خواند is the verb 'utter/recite' used with آفرین (to praise). و links the clauses and بر لشکرش همچنین means 'and likewise upon his army', where همچنین is 'similarly'.
segments.s290-g35: که‌ای is an archaic/exclamatory vocative ('O, you ...'); نامداران فرخنده are two adjectives in apposition — 'renowned, auspicious' — addressing the addressees. پی is used postpositively to mean 'after / in the wake of' and links to what follows (it governs the following noun phrase).
segments.s290-g36: This clause omits an explicit copula (typical in Persian): 'پی شما آتش' = 'after you (comes) fire' — شما is the focal pronoun, آتش 'fire' is the thing that follows. و links the second clause; خشک نی uses نی as an older negative particle following the adjective خشک ('dry'), together meaning 'not dry' — here idiomatically 'not left unharmed / not untroubled'.
segments.s290-g37: سپهدار گودرز is a nominal phrase: the title 'commander' followed by the proper name Goudarz. با دودمان uses با to mark accompaniment ('with his household/lineage'), so this phrase introduces who is acting or present.
segments.s290-g38: ز بهر is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of / on account of'; دل من 'my heart' states the beneficiary or motive. چو introduces a simile 'like/as', so چو آتش دمان is 'like a fire (آتش) at the dаман (skirt/hem)' — a poetic image. The clause as a whole gives purpose/motive ('for my heart') and a simile describing their ardent presence.
segments.s290-g39: همه جان و تن‌ها is a stock phrase 'all (their) lives and bodies' (i.e. everyone wholly). فدا کرده‌اند uses the set verb فدا کردن + perfective compound کرده‌اند to mean 'have devoted/sacrificed'. The compound indicates completed action with present relevance ('they have sacrificed').
segments.s290-g40: دم here is the noun 'breath' (used metonymically for a sound/cry). از شهر توران marks origin 'from the city of Turan'. برآورده‌اند (literally 'have brought forth') functions as 'they have uttered/raised' — so the clause means they have produced/raised a cry (breath) from Turan.
segments.s290-g121: ببد is an archaic past form of بودن ('was/remained'); here the verb precedes the subject (poetic inversion) and combines with شاه 'the king' to mean 'the king remained/was'. چندی is an adverb 'for a while'. بر آن رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'on that battlefield' (بر + demonstrative آن + noun رزمگاه).
segments.s290-g122: بدان تا is a purpose phrase: بدان (here = به + آن 'to/at that[place]' in a fused poetic form) + تا 'so that/in order that'. کند is the 3rd person singular subjunctive/present used after تا to express purpose ('that he may do'). ساز کار سپاه is an object/goal phrase 'the arranging/ordering (ساز کار) of the host (سپاه)'.
segments.s290-g123: دهد is the verb 'gives' (present/gnomic or narrative present in epic); پادشاهی is the object 'kingship'. که را is a relative clause marker plus the definite-object marker: که 'who/whom' with را marking it as the object of دهد. در خور است is a predicate 'is worthy/appropriate for (it)' — 'در خور' is an idiomatic classical phrase 'fit/appropriate'.
segments.s290-g124: کسی 'someone' begins a clarifying appositive; کز is an archaic fused form equivalent to که از 'who/from whom' introducing the relative descriptor. در here is the preposition 'in/within' (not 'door'), خلعت و افسر are the items (robe of honour and helmet) that mark a person as suitable; است closes the clause 'is'.
segments.s290-g125: به + indirect object گودرز = 'to Goudarz' (prepositional indirect object before the verb). داد is the past/narrative 'gave'. آن زمان اصفهان is an adverbial phrase 'at that time in Isfahan' (آن زمان = 'at that time', اصفهان = place). The phrase order is typical of epic style (indirect object, verb, temporal/place phrase).
segments.s290-g126: کلاه بزرگی is a noun + modifier 'a large/great hat' (بزرگی as size/greatness). و links the two gifts; تخت مهان is a compound 'throne of the guests' or 'the guests' throne' (تخت = throne, مهان = guests/visitors). This is a simple coordinate noun phrase listing the bestowed items.
segments.s290-g127: به اندازه اندرخور is an adverbial phrase 'to the appropriate measure / in proportion to what is fitting' (به اندازه = 'to the extent/measure', اندرخور = 'worthy/appropriate'); کارشان is the possessed noun 'their affairs/work'. The whole preposes the manner/degree for the following verb.
segments.s290-g128: بیاراست is a past-tense verbal form 'he arrayed/adorned/put in order' (ب‑ prefix + آراست). خلعت is the direct object 'robes of honour' and سزاوارشان is an adjectival/predicative phrase 'befitting/deserved by them' (سزاوار + possessive شان). Together: 'he adorned them with robes fitting to them.'
segments.s290-g113: که is the subordinating/complementizer 'that' introducing what follows as the content of a desire or intent. خون سیاوش is a possessive noun phrase with the possessed noun before the owner's name ("Siyâvash's blood"). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and links the object to the agent/source افراسیاب (Afrasiab). The whole segment functions as the objective clause whose obtaining is desired.
segments.s290-g114: بخواهم is a volitional/potential present-subjunctive form meaning 'I will / I intend' or 'I want to' in classical/poetic register; it expresses the speaker's resolve. بدین here should be read as 'with this' (به + این) and links to the following noun کینه as an instrumental/adverbial phrase giving motive; because the dataset segments the parts oddly, دین is not being used in its literal senses here. کینه is the motive 'grudge/hatred', and گیرم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive of گرفتن meaning 'I take/seize' (here 'I exact' or 'I take vengeance'). شتاب is an adverb/verb-root meaning 'to hasten' or 'with haste', modifying the action of گیرم.
segments.s290-g115: گروی is a poetic/archaic verbal/participial form here meaning 'they fastened / tied' (contextually 'had tied' or 'caused to be tied'); the given lemma senses include 'to join/adopt', so the sense used is the sense of joining/fastening. زره را is the direct object 'the armor' marked by the object particle را. گره تا گره is a repetitive adverbial phrase 'knot by knot' (گره = 'knot'; تا links the repetitions: '... to ... / ... by ...' used here as 'knot by knot').
segments.s290-g116: بفرمود is the (polite/honorific) past reporting verb 'he ordered/commanded'. تا here introduces a subordinate clause with purpose/result ('so that / in order that'), leading to برکشیدند 'they raised/drew' (perfective past plural), and زه is the object 'the bowstring'. Together: 'he commanded that they draw up the bowstring.'
segments.s290-g117: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بندش is a possessive noun (بند + -ش) meaning 'its/his fastening or tie' (the fastening that held something); جدا 'apart' plus شد 'became/was' form the predicate 'was separated / came apart'. سرش را is the definite object 'his head' (سر + possessive sh + object marker را). ز بند is a prepositional phrase 'from the bond/fastening' (ز = 'from', بند = 'bond/fetter/link'), specifying from where the head was severed.
segments.s290-g118: بریدند is the past plural 'they cut / they severed'. همچون is the comparative 'like / as'. سر is again 'head' and گوسفند is 'sheep'; the simile makes clear the manner of cutting: they cut the head off as one cuts a sheep's head.
segments.s290-g119: بفرمود is the past honorific/reporting verb 'he commanded'. او را is the direct object 'him' with the object marker را. فگندن (an older/poetic infinitive) means 'to throw' and takes به آب 'into the water' (به = prepositional 'into/towards', آب = 'water') — together: 'he ordered that he be thrown into the water.'
segments.s290-g120: بگفتا is the past narrative 'he said'. چنین is the adverb 'thus / so'. بینم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of 'to see' used here idiomatically as 'I see / I behold' (a present-tense commentary or exclamation). افراسیاب is the vocative/addressee 'Afrasiab' (the person spoken to). Grammatically this is a speech tag: he said to Afrasiab, 'Thus I see (it).'
segments.s290-g25: زرهٔ عبارت: "همه کشتگان را به خسرو نمود" is a past‑tense clause with the verb نمود (showed) at the end — a common classical Persian order (SOV). را is the definite/object marker attached to the direct object همه کشتگان; به introduces the indirect object (the person to whom the bodies were shown). خسرو is the indirect object (to Khosrow).
segments.s290-g26: بگفتش is بَ + گفت + ش (the enclitic -ش = “to him”), so “he said/told to him.” که introduces the reported content clause. The phrase همرزم هر کس که بود is a relative/free‑relative construction: همرزم is the predicate noun ‘comrade‑in‑arms’ and هر کس که بود (‘whoever/which person it was’) specifies whose comrade — i.e. “who had been each man’s comrade.” بود is the past copula.
segments.s290-g27: This clause gives the object and the bringing action: زره را (the armor) is marked by را as definite direct object and بیاورد is the past tense verb ‘brought.’ In this poetic word order the list of bringers follows the verb. گیو is a proper name (Giv) listed among the agents. گروی appears before زره and behaves as a modifier of the armor phrase (a compound/descriptor), placed pre‑nominally in the verse.
segments.s290-g28: This is a continuation of the agent list introduced by the verb: names and companions are linked by با ‘with.’ دمان and نیو are proper names here (listed as bringers), سپهدار پیران is a title + name construction where سپهدار is the title ‘commander’ and پیران is the proper name. با connects Daman with the commander Piran (and Niu) as joint agents.
segments.s290-g29: ز اسب is a poetic prepositional phrase ‘from (the) horse.’ اندر آمد (literally ‘came in/into’) is used here transitively to mean ‘dismounted’ or ‘came down’; this verbal phrase ends the clause. سبک (‘light’) modifies آمد and here functions adverbially (‘lightly’). شهریار is the subject (‘the king’) placed after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s290-g30: نیایش همی کرد is a verbal phrase meaning ‘he prayed / he offered prayers.’ همی is an archaic/epic continuous/habitual marker that combines with the past tense کرد to give a repeated or customary action. بر + کردگار = ‘to/onto the Creator’ (بر used as a dative/locative toward God).
segments.s290-g31: ز یزدان سپاس = ‘thanks to God’ (ز here is the poetic form of از). The conjunction و links the next verb phrase. بدویم پناه is a verbal phrase meaning ‘ran to/turned to (him) for refuge’ or more loosely ‘sought refuge in him;’ بدویم is from دویدن (‘to run/hasten’) used here idiomatically and پناه is the object ‘refuge/protection’. The sequence expresses giving thanks and seeking protection from God.
segments.s290-g32: که introduces an explanatory clause (‘for’ / ‘because’). او is the pronoun ‘He’ (referring to God). داد is the past verb ‘gave.’ پیروزی و دستگاه is the compound object: پیروزی ‘victory’ and دستگاه ‘establishment/authority/court’ — together meaning He granted victory and power/authority.
segments.s290-g57: ز خون سیاوش is a prepositional phrase (ز = poetic از = 'from/of') placed before the predicate; پر از درد is an adjectival phrase meaning 'full of pain/sorrow' (پر + از + noun). بود is the past copula linking the predicative phrase to the subject (understood). The whole chunk reads literally 'was full of pain from Siavash's blood.'
segments.s290-g58: بدانگه is a temporal adverb 'at that time/thereupon'. کسی را is the object marked by را (here کسی = 'anyone'). نیازرد is a negative past-form verb (نی- negative + آزار/آزاریدن 'to hurt'), so کسی را نیازرد بود = 'he did not hurt anyone'; بود is the past auxiliary/copula. Note the object marker را attaches to کسی and the negative prefix نی- makes نیازرد 'did not harm.'
segments.s290-g59: چنان مهربان بود is an intensifying clause: چنان ('so') + adjective + بود (past copula) = 'he was so kind'. The result clause is simple postposed: دژخیم شد ('became dzhakhim/oppressive/torturer-like') where شد is the past/resultative 'became'. The construction implies 'so … that …' with the usual comparative/result sense, the linking 'که' is omitted in poetry.
segments.s290-g60: وز او is an archaic poetic cluster (و + ز) meaning 'and/from him' or 'because of him'; شهر ایران is the noun phrase 'the city of Iran' with noun + genitive. پر از بیم شد is an adjectival/resultative phrase 'became full of fear' (پر از = 'full of', بیم = 'fear'), and شد is the past/resultative 'became'.
segments.s290-g61: مر is an archaic/object form corresponding to مرا ('me'); او را is the direct object 'him' (را marks definiteness/object). ببرد here carries the core meaning 'take away' (classical past/subjunctive/optative form often used for a wish/curse: 'may he take'), and the agent/name اهرمن (Ahriman/the devil) appears postverbally (poetic word order). دل ز جای is a verbless phrase 'heart from (its) place' beginning the following image; ز = 'from'.
segments.s290-g62: دگرگونه is an adverb/adjective 'in a different manner'; پیش 'forward/forwardly' modifies the motion; اندر آورد is a compound verb 'brought/put in/advanced' (اندر = 'in/into' + آورد 'brought'); پای is the object 'foot' placed at the end (typical Persian SOV order for verb-object in poetry). Together: 'he brought his foot forward in a different way.'
segments.s290-g63: فراوان همی are adverbial/intensifying particles ('abundantly/often' + continuous particle), modifying the clause that follows. خیره here functions as a manner adverb 'with a fixed stare' or 'vacantly/attentively' in poetic usage, and دادمش پند is 'I gave him advice' (دادم + ـش suffix 'to him' = 'I gave him'). Word order places manner and frequency before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s290-g64: نیامدش is a negative/resultive predicate with the clitic -ش 'to him' — literally 'it did not come to him' but used idiomatically 'did not avail him / was not effective for him'. گفتار من is the subject noun phrase 'my speech/words' and سودمند is the predicate adjective 'useful'. The verb precedes the subject here (inverted order) as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s290-g65: از + افراسیابش is a prepositional phrase ('from Afrasiab...'). The clitic -ش attached to افراسیاب is the third-person possessive/object clitic. نه before the verb برگشت is the negator, so نه برگشت (literally 'did not return') is a negated past action; سر (here 'head') follows and functions as the noun affected by the verb (word order: prepositional phrase → negation + verb → object).
segments.s290-g66: کنون is an adverb 'now'. شهریارش is شهریار ('king') with the third-person clitic -ش ('his/its'); the clitic marks relation/possession and is common on titles. چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. داد is the simple past of دادن ('gave/acted'); with the following preposition بر it forms a classical collocation meaning roughly 'dealt upon/inflicted/acted against'. بر here is the preposition linking the action to its target.
segments.s290-g67: مکافات او is a noun phrase 'the recompense/retribution of him' (مکافات + clitic او). ما جز این خواستیم is the main clause: ما (we) + جز این (except this) placed before the past verb خواستیم (we wanted/asked for). The verb خواستیم is 1st-person plural past; the sentence asserts they wanted nothing as recompense except what follows.
segments.s290-g68: همی is an emphatic/classical adverb (here 'ever'/'constantly'). گاه literally 'time' stands here as part of a paired phrase. و is the conjunction 'and'. دیهیمش is the noun دیهیم (related to 'دیه/دِه' = village; here with the possessive clitic -ش) — so 'his village/estate' or a rustic retinue. آراستیم is 1st-person plural past of آراستن ('we adorned/arranged/supplied'); the clause means they continually provided or adorned him with place and village.
segments.s290-g69: از اندیشه ما is a genitive phrase 'from our thought/intention' (از + noun + clitic/possessive). سخن درگذشت uses سخن ('a word/speech') with درگذشت (past of گذشت) to mean 'a word passed/slipped (out)' — a classical idiom signalling that something was spoken or left the mind unexpectedly. درگذشت is here a past action.
segments.s290-g70: فلک here is used in the abstract/poetic sense 'fate/destiny' rather than literal sky. بر + سرش ('upon his head') marks the domain affected by that fate; ش is the possessive clitic 'his'. دگرگونه گشت is a verbal phrase: دگرگونه ('differently/changed') + گشت (past of گشتن 'became/turned'), so 'fate turned/changed in a different way upon him.'
segments.s290-g71: به دل بر جفا کرد is built around کرد (past of کردن 'did/caused'): به دل ('to/into the heart') + بر جفا کردن ('to do cruelty/injustice upon'); the structure means 'he made his heart cruel / he hardened his heart with cruelty.' بر جای مهر places one thing in the stead of another: بر جای + noun ('in place of') مهر ('love/affection'), so 'in place of affection.'
segments.s290-g72: بدین (here 'with this' or 'thus') is classical/deictic; in the manuscript the element is split into ب + دین, where ب is the verbal/prepositional prefix and دین is the deictic element (in this gloss دین does not mean 'religion' and is better read as part of بدین). سر ('face/head' here idiomatically) + دگرگونه ('different/changed') modify بنمود (past of بنمودن = نمودن 'showed/displayed'), and چهر ('face/visage') is the object: 'thus he put on/displayed a changed face.' بنمود is an archaic past form equivalent to 'نمود'/'نشان داد'.
segments.s290-g89: The phrase is an adverbial/prepositional phrase + noun phrase placed before the verb (poetic SOV inversion). به دیبای رومی = “in/by Roman brocade”; به here marks manner/means. تن پاک اوی is a noun phrase meaning “her pure body” with پاک as an adjective before the noun and اوی as a possessive pronoun attached (her).
segments.s290-g90: بپوشید is a past tense 3rd-person singular verb (پوشیدن, with poetic prefix ب-), here meaning either “dressed” or “covered/veiled.” آن جان ناپاک اوی is the definite object: آن = “that,” جان = “soul,” ناپاک = “impure,” and اوی again a possessive “her/his.” The verb therefore applies to the following object (he/she hid/covered that impure soul).
segments.s290-g91: Word order is object–verb–subject (poetic inversion): یکی دخمه = “a certain tomb,” فرمود is past tense “ordered/commanded/said,” خسرو is the subject (Khosrow) and به مهر is an adverbial phrase “with affection/with favour.”
segments.s290-g92: بر آورده is a compound verbal phrase: بر + آورده (past participle/verb) = “lifted/raised up.” سر is the direct object “head.” تا به گردان سپهر = a simile/comparative phrase “up to/as far as the revolving heavens,” where تا marks extent and گردان سپهر is a genitive phrase (“revolving sky/heavens”).
segments.s290-g93: نهاد is here the past form of نهادن (“placed/put”), followed by اندر او (“in/within it”), then تختهای گران = “heavy/precious thrones.” The verb precedes the locative phrase (typical of poetic ordering): “he placed inside it thrones heavy/valuable.”
segments.s290-g94: چنانچون بود is a comparative/copular clause “just as/so it was,” where بود is the past copula. در خور مهتران is a predicate prepositional phrase: در خور = “fitting/appropriate for” and مهتران = “the great/elderly/high-ranking ones.” The whole clause means “as was fitting for great men.”
segments.s290-g95: نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن “they placed/set.” مر is the enclitic me/to me (“for me” or “my” depending on context), پهلوان را is the definite object marked by را (“the champion”), and به گاه is a temporal phrase “at the time/at the appointed hour.” The clause means “they placed/set my champion at the time.”
segments.s290-g96: This is a pair of prepositional noun phrases giving the accoutrements: کمر بر میان = “a girdle/belt at the middle” (i.e. round his waist) and به سر بر کلاه = “and on the head a cap.” Note the poetic inversion: object phrases appear before/around the nouns they modify, and repeated prepositions (بر/به بر) mark location.
segments.s290-g41: کنون = initial adverb meaning “now.” گنج و شاهی is the coordinated subject (“treasure and kingship”). مرا is an older/genitive/pronominal clitic attached here to indicate possession (“of me / my”); in classical Persian it appears as م + را. با شما is a prepositional phrase “with you,” and ست is the final copula (“is”). Word order: Adverb → subject → possessive clitic → prepositional phrase → copula.
segments.s290-g42: ندارم is first-person present (ن + دارم) “I do not have / I am not possessed by.” دریغ از شما is a noun phrase “reluctance/hesitation concerning you” (دریغ = reluctance; از + شما marks the relation). دست راست (“right hand”) is the concrete thing referred to; the phrase is elliptical for “I have no hesitation in giving you my right hand.”
segments.s290-g43: از آن پس is an adverbial phrase “after that / thereafter” (از = “from,” آن = “that,” پس = “after”). بدان here is an archaic/clitic sequence (ب = be-/to + دان/آن) meaning “to those / at them.” کشتگان is the object “the slain.” بنگرید is a classical narrative verb form meaning “looked / gazed” (here past narrative). Word order: temporal phrase → directional pronoun → object → verb.
segments.s290-g44: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” روی سپهدار پیران is a genitival/nominal group: روی = “face,” سپهدار = “the (army) commander,” پیران here is the name/title (Piran); together “the face of the commander Piran.” بدید is simple past third-person “saw.” The clause is a temporal subordinate: “when he saw the face of Piran the commander.”
segments.s290-g45: فروریخت آب is a poetic expression “water (i.e., tears) poured down.” از دو دیده = “from the two eyes” (داری/دو omitted in words list but implied). به درد = “with sorrow / in anguish” (به + درد indicating manner or state). The verb is pictorial: literal pouring of water from the eyes = weeping.
segments.s290-g46: که introduces a content clause “that.” کردار نیکی is “a good deed / good conduct” (کردار = deed; نیکی qualifies it). همی is the classical continuous/iterative particle often used with past narrative to mark ongoing or habitual action (here functioning like an imperfective marker in older Persian). یاد کرد is a compound meaning “remembered” (یاد = remembrance, کرد = did → ‘made remembrance’ = remembered).
segments.s290-g47: به پیرانش = to/for his elders (به introducing the beneficiary/target; پیران + ش = “his elders” or those he reveres). بر دل = literally “upon the heart” (locative expression meaning emotionally). از آن سان = “in that manner / so,” and بسوخت is past intransitive/affective “burned” (figurative: his heart was consumed with grief/passion).
segments.s290-g48: تو is the second-person pronoun “you” (here either direct address or narrative vocative). گفتی is the past-second-person form “you said” (used narratively here). به دلش = “in his heart” (به + دل + ش = to/in his heart). آتشی is a noun/adjective “a fire / fiery (feeling)” and برفروخت is past “kindled / inflamed” (here figurative: a fire was kindled in his heart).

sentences.s290-s65: Among those who had remained in their places, their elders were ill‑tempered and the village headman was bad.
sentences.s290-s53: He kept saying, "O Maker of the world, you know the manifest and the hidden."
sentences.s290-s54: Truly, because Kavus had done ill, in return he experienced from him poison and enmity.
sentences.s290-s55: That a demon thus set (it) upon Siavash — I know not, apart from this, what grudge he bore in his heart.
sentences.s290-s56: But, by the victory of the one God who rules the world, grant goodness and be my guide.
sentences.s290-s37: Now he cast aside Goudarz's counsel and my command, discarding my words and my pledge.
sentences.s290-s38: He perverted (or ruined) the affection of the pure‑hearted and mixed opium into the poison.
sentences.s290-s39: who came against you to battle with an army, so great was the destruction that befell the cities of Iran.
sentences.s290-s40: They mustered from Turan and came to the plain; at that time Goudarz wore his gambeson.
sentences.s290-s5: Thus he remained on the king's horse, so that the army might see his face.
sentences.s290-s6: The king kept calling out 'Bravo!' to them, saying, 'May the land be prosperous by the host.'
